title.en: Guarding Your Speech (Shmirat HaLashon): The Laws of Lashon Hara
title.zh: 谨守口舌 (Shmirat HaLashon):恶言的律法

sources.title: Chofetz Chaim
sources.author: Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan
sources.description: Definitive work on the laws of proper speech

sources.title: Talmud Bavli, Tractate Arachin 15b
sources.description: The severity of lashon hara compared to the three cardinal sins

sources.title: Proverbs 18:21
sources.description: Death and life are in the power of the tongue

chinese.translation: Illness enters through the mouth; disaster comes out of it
chinese.relevance: Parallels Jewish teaching about the power and danger of speech
Echoes Talmudic wisdom about the value of silence

chinese.translation: Be cautious in speech and prudent in action
chinese.relevance: Direct parallel to shmirat halashon concept

chinese.translation: Once a word is spoken, even four horses cannot retrieve it

chinese.proverb: 良言一句三冬暖,恶语伤人六月寒
chinese.translation: One kind word warms three winters; harsh words hurt even in the sixth month
chinese.relevance: Perfectly embodies Jewish teaching about the power of words

chinese.concept: 君子欲讷于言而敏于行 (Analects)
chinese.translation: The superior person is cautious in speech and diligent in action
chinese.relevance: Confucian ideal aligns with Jewish emphasis on guarded speech

chinese.translation: Confucian virtue of benevolence in both action and speech
chinese.relevance: Similar to Jewish ethics emphasizing speech as action with consequences

practicalApplications: Three questions before speaking: Is it true? Is it necessary? Is it kind?
author: Torah Light Team
